Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Varela, Thompson & Rosch

late 20th c.

The Embodied Mind: enactivism, autopoiesis, and neurophenomenology.

Varela, Thompson, and Rosch’s deepest claim is that mind is not an inner theater, not a disembodied rational faculty, and not fundamentally a representational computer lodged in the head. Mind is the ongoing, embodied, environmentally embedded, historically conditioned activity through which living beings enact domains of significance. Cognition is not primarily the mirroring of a pre-given world by a pre-given subject; it is the “bringing forth” of a meaningful world through embodied action. This does not mean that organisms invent reality arbitrarily. It means that what counts as salient, relevant, threatening, edible, graspable, true, false, self, other, object, tool, person, and world is disclosed through the structured coupling of organism and environment.

The revised MIT Press description of The Embodied Mind says that the book introduced “enaction” as a form of cognitive science in which environment and first-person experience are both aspects of embodiment; it also says that enactive embodiment is not the grasping of an independent outside world by an independent brain, mind, or self, but the bringing forth of an interdependent world through embodied action. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy similarly describes enaction as the bringing forth of domains of significance through organismic activity conditioned by a history of organism-environment interaction.

The resulting philosophy of mind can be named, with some compression, enactive embodied phenomenological naturalism. It is naturalistic because it insists that mind belongs to living nature, not to a separate mental substance. It is non-reductive because it refuses to identify mind simply with neural mechanisms described from the third-person point of view. It is phenomenological because lived experience is not an optional gloss on cognition but part of the target domain. It is enactive because organism and world are understood through dynamic co-constitution rather than through a one-way relation of input, internal representation, and output.

2. Historical position in philosophy of mind

VTR enter the philosophy of mind after several major twentieth-century phases. Behaviorism tried to make mind scientifically respectable by avoiding inner experience. Identity theory and reductive materialism identified mental states with brain states. Functionalism then shifted the focus from material constitution to causal role: a mental state is what it does within a system, not what it is made of. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy defines functionalism in precisely this way: mental states are individuated by their functional roles in a system rather than by their internal constitution.

The classical cognitive-science revolution replaced behaviorism with computationalism. On the computational theory of mind, core mental processes such as reasoning, decision-making, and problem-solving are computations, often modeled on Turing-style symbol manipulation. SEP notes that this computational theory played a central role in cognitive science in the 1960s and 1970s and long enjoyed orthodox status. Connectionism later challenged classical symbolic computation by modeling cognitive capacities through artificial neural networks rather than explicit rules and symbols, though it often retained the idea that cognition is still a kind of internal information processing.

VTR’s intervention is historically decisive because it is neither a return to behaviorism nor a mere improvement of computationalism. It is part of the emergence of what is now called embodied, embedded, enacted, and extended cognition, or “4E” cognition. SEP describes embodied cognition as a broad research program that rejects or reformulates traditional computational commitments and emphasizes the constitutive or contributory role of the body and body-environment interaction in cognition. The Google Books description of the revised edition calls The Embodied Mind a classic work that originated the embodied cognition movement and helped connect phenomenology, science, Buddhist practices, and cognitive science.

Historically, then, VTR are best understood as a third major wave after classical computational cognitivism and connectionism. They accept from cognitivism that mind can be scientifically investigated. They accept from connectionism that cognition is distributed, emergent, and not controlled by a central homunculus. But they argue that both remain incomplete if they neglect embodiment, lived experience, selflessness, action, and the organism’s meaningful world.

3. The problem they are trying to solve

The central problem is not merely “How does the brain produce mental representations?” but rather: how can a science of mind account for lived experience without either reducing it away or treating it as a private, non-scientific mystery?

The MIT Press description states that VTR argue for a common ground between mind in science and mind in experience; their project develops a dialogue among cognitive science, Buddhist meditative psychology, phenomenology, and psychoanalysis. The IEP describes original enactivism as motivated by the problem of how lived experience fits into the world described by science, and it says VTR sought a way for cognitive science to give proper attention to lived experience.

Their diagnosis is that modern cognitive science inherited a hidden Cartesian structure even when it rejected Cartesian dualism. It still often assumed an inner subject that receives information from an outer world, constructs representations, and then issues behavioral outputs. VTR argue that this structure is wrong at both ends. There is no self-sufficient inner ego that owns cognition from the inside, and there is no fully pre-given environment whose significance is simply copied into the mind from the outside. Subject and object are not independent starting points. They are relational achievements.

This is why their project is not just a theory of cognition but a philosophical reorientation. They are attacking a whole family of dualisms: mind/body, subject/object, internal/external, fact/value, science/experience, representation/action, cognition/affect, organism/environment, and theory/practice.

4. The three authors and their distinct contributions

Francisco Varela supplies the biological and systems-theoretic foundation. His earlier work with Humberto Maturana on autopoiesis defines living systems as self-producing, self-maintaining, self-individuating systems. In the enactive tradition, this becomes the basis for a life-mind continuity thesis: cognition is rooted in the self-organizing activity of living beings. The IEP notes that original enactivism takes biological autonomy as central and sees deep continuity between life and mind.

Evan Thompson supplies much of the phenomenological and philosophical articulation. His later work makes explicit what is already present in The Embodied Mind: mind must be studied through a disciplined relation between biology, phenomenology, and cognitive science. Thompson later described the book’s aim as cross-fertilization between cognitive science and the phenomenology of human experience, saying that VTR reconceptualized cognition as embodied action and named this the “enactive approach.”

Eleanor Rosch supplies a crucial psychological background. Her work on categorization and prototypes had already undermined the classical idea that concepts are normally defined by necessary and sufficient conditions. SEP notes that prototype theory has psychological roots in Rosch’s experimental work and explains typicality effects better than the classical theory of concepts. This matters for VTR because Rosch’s psychology supports a view of cognition as situated, graded, embodied, pragmatic, and world-involving rather than purely logical or formal.

Taken together, Varela brings autonomy and living systems; Thompson brings phenomenology and philosophy of mind; Rosch brings cognitive psychology and the critique of classical categories. The synthesis is not accidental. Their philosophy of mind is biological, phenomenological, psychological, and contemplative at once.

5. Critique of cognitivism

For VTR, cognitivism is the view that cognition is fundamentally the manipulation of internal representations according to formal rules. The world is assumed to be already structured; the mind receives inputs from it, builds representations of it, and computes over those representations. This picture is powerful, especially for formal reasoning, language processing, and artificial intelligence. But VTR think it mistakes a derivative cognitive capacity for the essence of mind.

Their critique has several layers.

First, cognitivism is too internalist. It treats cognition as something that happens inside the head and treats body and environment as input-output channels. VTR argue instead that the body’s sensorimotor organization is constitutive of cognition.

Second, cognitivism is too representationalist. It assumes that the primary relation between mind and world is correspondence between inner symbols and outer facts. VTR argue that the primary relation is embodied coping, orientation, and sense-making.

Third, cognitivism is too objectivist. It assumes that the world is given independently with ready-made structures waiting to be represented. VTR argue that meaningful worlds are enacted by living beings with specific bodily capacities and histories.

Fourth, cognitivism is phenomenologically incomplete. It may explain task performance, but it cannot by itself explain what it is like to perceive, act, suffer, attend, meditate, remember, or inhabit a world. Varela’s later neurophenomenology makes this point explicit: he describes neurophenomenology as an attempt to join modern cognitive science with a disciplined approach to human experience.

The target, then, is not computation as a useful tool. The target is the metaphysical elevation of computation into the essence of mind.

6. Critique of connectionism and emergence

VTR are more sympathetic to connectionism than to classical cognitivism because connectionism emphasizes distributed processing, emergence, and self-organization. It also weakens the picture of the mind as a centralized symbol-manipulating machine. But VTR think connectionism remains insufficient if it is still interpreted as an internal representational architecture.

Connectionism shows that cognitive processes can arise from distributed networks without a central controller. This helps destabilize the idea of a unified ego-self. But emergence alone does not yet explain embodiment, lived experience, or meaningful world-disclosure. A neural network can be self-organizing, but the philosophical question remains: how does a living, embodied being inhabit a world of significance?

Thus, connectionism is an intermediate step. It breaks with classical symbolic mentalism, but enaction goes further. It moves the basic unit of analysis from brain-internal processing to the dynamic system composed of brain, body, action, world, history, and experience.

7. The enactive alternative

The enactive view can be formulated in one main thesis: cognition is embodied sense-making.

A living being is not a passive receiver of stimuli. It is an autonomous system that maintains itself by interacting with its environment. Because it must maintain itself, the world matters to it. Things show up as food, obstacle, danger, mate, shelter, tool, rival, companion, sign, or question. This “mattering” is not added after perception by a detached intellect. It is built into the organism’s practical relation to its world.

The IEP says enactivists reject the idea that organisms merely adapt to a pre-existing, neutrally described physical world; instead, organisms are attuned to environments or domains significant to them, and these domains are brought forth through organismic activity. Mind and Life’s explanation of the enactive approach gives the same core idea: cognition is not representation of an independent world by an independent mind, but the enactment of a world of significance through embodied action.

This is not subjective idealism. VTR do not claim that the organism fabricates the physical universe. They distinguish the physical world from an organism’s enacted environment, or meaningful Umwelt. Sucrose exists chemically, but it is “food” only in relation to an organism whose metabolism can use it. A stone exists physically, but it becomes obstacle, tool, weapon, landmark, or sacred object only within forms of life.

So the enactive alternative is a middle path: against objectivism, there is no meaningful world wholly independent of embodied sense-making; against subjectivism, sense-making is constrained by material, biological, historical, and intersubjective regularities.

8. Embodiment in the strong sense

In weak embodiment, the body influences cognition. In strong embodiment, the body partly constitutes cognition. VTR defend the stronger view. A mind is not a brain that happens to be housed in a body. A mind is a living body’s way of being in a world.

The body matters in several ways. It provides sensorimotor capacities; it structures perception through movement and posture; it generates affective valence through metabolism, homeostasis, and vulnerability; it situates the organism in a spatial and temporal world; and it gives cognition its practical orientation. Vision, for example, is not merely the registration of visual inputs. It is an active skill of looking, moving, orienting, anticipating, and exploring.

The enactive approach later becomes one strand within the larger 4E family: cognition is embodied, embedded, extended, and enacted. Mind and Life summarizes this by saying cognition depends on the body as a functional whole, is embedded in the environment, and is performed by the coupled brain-body-environment system. SEP similarly treats enactive cognition as a major strand of embodied cognition, with autopoietic enactivism rooted in the biodynamics of living systems.

The strong claim is that we cannot first define cognition internally and then add body and environment as accessories. The unit of analysis must be the embodied agent in action.

9. Perception: not inner representation but skillful access

In VTR’s philosophy of perception, perceiving is not the construction of an inner picture. It is an embodied ability to navigate the world. Perception is active, exploratory, and world-involving.

This does not mean that the brain does nothing. The brain is indispensable. But the brain’s role is not to generate a private internal world that the subject then contemplates. Rather, the brain participates in a larger organism-environment system. Perception is the enacted relation between embodied capacities and environmental affordances.

Color perception is a paradigmatic case for VTR and Thompson. Color is not simply a wavelength “out there,” nor a private quale “in here.” It is a relational phenomenon involving lighting, surfaces, visual systems, bodily capacities, ecological relevance, and perceptual practices. Thompson later noted that color perception was one of the central examples of enaction in The Embodied Mind.

This view is neither naive realism nor sense-data theory. It is relational realism: perceptual qualities are real, but their reality is relationally enacted rather than object-intrinsic or subject-private.

10. Intentionality as sense-making

A traditional problem in philosophy of mind asks how mental states can be “about” things. How can a brain state refer to a tree, a number, a promise, or a possible future? Classical cognitivism answers through internal representations. VTR answer through sense-making.

The primitive form of intentionality is not symbolic aboutness but organismic directedness. A bacterium moving along a chemical gradient is already oriented toward a world that matters to its self-maintenance. This is not yet conceptual thought, and it is not yet reflective consciousness. But it is a basic form of world-directed normativity: some interactions support viability, others threaten it.

From this perspective, semantic intentionality grows out of biological and practical intentionality. Meaning is rooted in relevance, and relevance is rooted in embodied life. The mind is about the world because the living body is already involved in a world that matters.

This is one of VTR’s most important contributions: they attempt to naturalize intentionality without reducing it to mere causal registration and without invoking a detached representational subject.

11. Selfhood and no-self

VTR’s treatment of the self is one of the book’s philosophical centers. They argue against the idea of a substantial, unified, self-identical ego that stands behind experience as its owner. Cognitive science, connectionism, psychoanalysis, phenomenology, and Buddhist psychology all converge, in different ways, on the thought that the self is not a simple entity.

The point is not that persons do not exist at all. The point is that the self is not an independently existing substance. It is a pattern, process, construction, performance, and practical designation. It emerges through bodily regulation, memory, affect, social recognition, language, and narrative. The self is real as an enacted pattern, not as a metaphysical core.

This is where Buddhist no-self doctrine becomes central. VTR use Buddhist analyses of the person into aggregates, mental factors, and dependent arising to show that what we call “self” is a dependently arisen organization rather than an inner owner. MIT’s description explicitly says the book develops a dialogue with Buddhist meditative psychology, and the revised edition description says it links Buddhist practices with cognitive science.

The philosophical result is a middle way between substantialism and nihilism. Substantialism says there is an enduring ego-entity. Nihilism says there is no self in any sense, so agency, responsibility, and continuity collapse. VTR reject both. There is no ultimate ego, but there are embodied persons, histories, responsibilities, and transformations.

12. Consciousness and first-person method

VTR do not treat consciousness as an embarrassing residue. They treat it as central. A science of mind that cannot account for lived experience is incomplete because lived experience is part of what mind is.

Varela later developed this into neurophenomenology. His 1996 article, Neurophenomenology: A Methodological Remedy for the Hard Problem, proposes a research program that joins cognitive science with disciplined first-person investigation. The key claim is methodological rather than mystical: first-person experience must be studied rigorously, trained, described, and correlated with neurobiological dynamics. Varela argues that neither isolated empirical correlates nor purely theoretical principles are enough; the structure of human experience itself must become part of the research program.

This is a direct response to the explanatory gap. VTR do not solve the hard problem by declaring consciousness identical to neural activity. Nor do they posit a nonphysical consciousness-stuff. Instead, they change the method. The third-person science of mind must enter into disciplined circulation with first-person phenomenology.

This is one of their most radical proposals for psychology: introspection should not be dismissed, but it must be transformed. Ordinary introspection is unstable, culturally mediated, and often confused. But disciplined phenomenological and contemplative practices may refine attention, description, and experiential discrimination.

13. Psychology as transformation, not only explanation

VTR’s psychology is not merely descriptive or predictive. It is also transformative. This is part of what makes their position unusual in mainstream philosophy of mind.

Modern psychology often treats the subject as an object of explanation: a system that perceives, remembers, categorizes, chooses, and behaves. VTR accept the need for objective science, but they also insist that human beings can transform their experience through disciplined practice. Mindfulness, phenomenological reflection, therapy, and ethical cultivation are not external applications of a theory of mind. They are part of the domain to be understood.

This is why the subtitle Cognitive Science and Human Experience matters. Their question is not just “What mechanisms produce cognition?” It is also “How can cognitive science matter for the conduct and transformation of life?” The book’s official description says it treats the spontaneous and reflective dimensions of human experience and tries to establish common ground between scientific mind and experiential mind.

A VTR-style psychology would therefore study perception, memory, emotion, categorization, selfhood, and consciousness in relation to bodily life, cultural practice, and possible transformation. The subject is not a passive container of mental states but an embodied practice of world-involvement.

14. Rosch, categorization, and concepts

Rosch’s role is especially important for extracting a complete philosophy of mind. Her prototype theory challenges the classical view that concepts are defined by necessary and sufficient conditions. Many categories have graded membership: robin is a more typical bird than penguin; chair is a more typical furniture item than lamp; some cases are borderline.

SEP notes that prototype theory explains typicality effects and has psychological roots in Rosch’s experimental work. This matters philosophically because it undermines the image of cognition as primarily formal classification. Human concepts are not usually detached logical definitions. They are embodied, pragmatic, perceptually structured, culturally learned, and action-guiding.

Rosch’s earlier work also supports the importance of “basic-level” categories: categories such as chair, dog, car, and cup are often more cognitively basic than highly abstract superordinates like furniture or animal, or highly specific subordinates like Windsor chair or golden retriever. Basic-level categories are tied to bodily interaction: what we can grasp, sit on, avoid, eat, draw, recognize, or use.

In an enactive philosophy of mind, concepts are not merely mental labels. They are modes of skilled orientation. A concept is a way of taking up the world, discriminating relevance, and coordinating action with others. Formal definition is a late, reflective, culturally sophisticated achievement built on more basic embodied categorization.

15. Emotion, affect, and value

Although The Embodied Mind is often discussed in relation to perception and selfhood, its philosophy implies a powerful account of affect. If cognition is sense-making by a living being, then affect is not a secondary coloring added to neutral cognition. Affect is the felt dimension of relevance.

Living systems are vulnerable. They can flourish or fail. Because of that, the world shows up with valence: attractive, aversive, urgent, boring, threatening, inviting. Emotion is therefore not irrational noise interfering with cognition. It is a mode of embodied appraisal and orientation.

This aligns VTR with later embodied and enactive work on emotion: emotions are not private inner episodes but whole-body, world-involving patterns of readiness, concern, and sense-making. Fear is not merely an internal state representing danger; it is a bodily reorganization of the field into escape routes, threats, obstacles, and possibilities. Anger, grief, joy, anxiety, and compassion similarly disclose worlds.

A full VTR philosophy of psychology therefore rejects the cognition/emotion divide. Cognition is affectively valenced from the start because living cognition is organized around what matters.

16. Intersubjectivity and social cognition

VTR’s original work already points beyond individual cognition, and later enactivism develops this strongly. The mind is not only embodied; it is interpersonal. Human cognition develops through caregivers, gesture, imitation, joint attention, language, ritual, education, and shared practices.

Mind and Life describes “participatory sense-making” as a social extension of enactive cognition: individuals directly participate in one another’s sense-making, and parent-infant interaction co-regulates physiology and supports the development of self-experience.

This gives VTR’s position a distinctive account of social cognition. We do not first infer hidden mental states from external behavior as detached observers. Much interpersonal understanding is direct, embodied, affective, and interactive. We perceive anger in a face, hesitation in a gesture, invitation in a posture, grief in a voice. Social understanding is enacted in the relational dynamics between bodies.

This does not deny that theory, inference, and simulation sometimes matter. It denies that they are the foundation of social cognition.

17. Language and symbolic thought

One possible objection to VTR is that embodied action may explain perception and motor skill, but not abstract thought, mathematics, logic, science, or philosophy. A complete reconstruction must address this.

The best enactive answer is that symbolic thought is real but derivative. Language is not an escape from embodiment; it is an embodied, social, historically accumulated practice. Symbols are not mental atoms floating in a private inner language. They are public tools used by embodied agents. A formula, map, diagram, legal concept, or philosophical distinction is an artifact within shared practices.

So VTR need not deny representation altogether. A mature enactive philosophy can say: representations exist, but they are not the metaphysical foundation of mind. They are enacted instruments within embodied, social activity. Scientific models, mathematical notations, and linguistic descriptions are powerful cognitive tools, but their intelligibility depends on practices of use.

This position differs from radical anti-representationalism if radical anti-representationalism says all talk of representation must be eliminated. VTR’s stronger and more defensible claim is that representation is not the basic form of cognition.

18. Groundlessness and the “middle way”

One of VTR’s most philosophical themes is groundlessness. They reject the demand for an absolute foundation: a self-grounding subject, a purely objective world, an indubitable given, or a final metaphysical standpoint. This is influenced by Madhyamaka Buddhism, especially the critique of intrinsic existence.

But groundlessness is not nihilism. The lack of ultimate foundations does not mean that anything goes. We still have embodied constraints, biological needs, communal practices, empirical accountability, ethical consequences, and pragmatic success or failure.

The revised edition description says that enacted cognition lacks an absolute foundation but that this need not lead to experiential or philosophical nihilism. This is essential. Their philosophy is not skeptical despair. It is a discipline of living without metaphysical guarantees.

The “middle way” is therefore epistemological, ontological, and ethical. Epistemologically, knowledge is neither a mirror of nature nor arbitrary construction. Ontologically, persons and objects are neither self-subsistent substances nor mere illusions. Ethically, selflessness need not destroy responsibility; it can support compassion by loosening egocentric fixation.

19. Relation to phenomenology

VTR explicitly inherit from Merleau-Ponty the idea that mind must be understood from the standpoint of embodied being-in-the-world. Phenomenology gives them the tools to criticize both objectivism and intellectualism. Experience is not a set of inner objects observed by a detached subject. It is world-involvement.

Yet VTR’s relation to phenomenology is complicated. In the original book, they sometimes presented Western phenomenology as having broken down because it lacked a disciplined transformative method comparable to Buddhist mindfulness. Thompson later corrected this. In a 2017 retrospective, he said he no longer accepted the book’s portrayal of Husserlian and Merleau-Pontian phenomenology as a failed project, and he emphasized that phenomenology remains vital for philosophy, cognitive science, medicine, the arts, and practices of transformation.

This later correction strengthens the reconstructed philosophy. The best version of VTR does not oppose phenomenology and Buddhism; it treats both as resources for disciplined attention to experience, while also recognizing their historical and methodological differences.

20. Relation to Buddhism

Buddhist thought is not ornamental in VTR. It supplies three major philosophical resources: no-self, dependent arising, and groundlessness or emptiness.

No-self helps dismantle the substantial ego. Dependent arising helps explain persons, experiences, and worlds as relational processes. Emptiness helps avoid both objectivism and subjectivism by denying intrinsic, independent existence to subject and object alike.

However, Thompson later criticized aspects of the original presentation. He said VTR’s depiction of Abhidharma and Madhyamaka as directly based on meditation was “simplistic and inaccurate,” because Buddhist philosophy was shaped by many factors, including doctrine, scholastic debate, and responses to non-Buddhist philosophers. He also warned against treating mindfulness as an inward observation that reveals an observer-independent mental stream; a better conception is to understand mindfulness as a skillful way of enacting embodied states and behaviors.

This is an important caveat. A rigorous VTR philosophy of mind should not claim that Buddhism is simply an ancient cognitive science. Rather, Buddhist philosophy and contemplative practice provide conceptual and practical resources that can enter into critical dialogue with cognitive science and phenomenology.

21. Relation to neurophenomenology

Neurophenomenology is Varela’s later methodological development of the same project. The basic idea is that first-person experiential reports and third-person neural data should constrain and illuminate each other.

Varela’s proposal is not ordinary introspection plus brain scans. It requires trained attention, careful phenomenological description, experimental discipline, and reciprocal constraints between experience and neurodynamics. In another text, Varela describes the aim as “mutual enlightenment” between first-person and third-person perspectives.

This gives VTR a distinctive response to consciousness science. They do not say, “Ignore experience and study the brain.” They also do not say, “Ignore the brain and trust introspection.” They say that consciousness requires a new kind of disciplined circulation between experiential and neurobiological investigation.

This is one of their most important contributions to contemporary philosophy of mind because it reframes the hard problem as partly a methodological crisis. The gap between consciousness and nature cannot be closed by third-person data alone if the experiential side remains untrained and theoretically crude.

22. Relation to 4E cognition and later enactivism

VTR are now considered foundational for enactivism. The IEP calls The Embodied Mind the locus classicus of enactivism and says it gave the first original contemporary formulation of the view. SEP distinguishes several contemporary strands: autopoietic enactivism, sensorimotor enactivism, and radical enactivism.

Autopoietic enactivism is closest to Varela and Thompson. It emphasizes life, autonomy, self-maintenance, biological normativity, and the continuity between life and mind. Sensorimotor enactivism, associated especially with O’Regan and Noë, focuses on perception as mastery of sensorimotor contingencies. Radical enactivism, associated with Hutto and Myin, is more aggressively anti-representational.

VTR are broader than all three. Their original project includes biological autonomy, perception, lived experience, Buddhist thought, selfhood, ethics, and the transformation of cognitive science. So it is misleading to reduce them to a narrow slogan such as “perception is action” or “representations do not exist.” Their work is a comprehensive reorientation of mind science.

23. Relation to extended mind

The extended mind thesis, associated with Clark and Chalmers, argues that cognitive processes can include external tools and artifacts. VTR’s view is related but not identical.

Extended mind asks: where does the mind stop? Enactivism asks: how is a meaningful world enacted by embodied activity? Extended mind often focuses on notebooks, tools, technologies, and external cognitive scaffolds. Enactivism focuses on the organism-world coupling that makes significance possible in the first place.

The two views can be combined, but VTR are more radical in one respect and less liberal in another. They are more radical because they challenge the subject-object structure underlying internalism. They are less liberal because they do not necessarily say that every useful external artifact becomes part of the mind. What matters is the dynamic, normative, embodied system of sense-making.

24. What philosophy of mind can be extracted?

The most comprehensive reconstruction would include the following theses.

First, mind is embodied activity. Mental life is not located in a disembodied subject or reducible to a brain considered in isolation. Brain processes are necessary for human cognition, but they function within the living body’s active engagement with the world.

Second, cognition is sense-making. To cognize is to enact a domain of significance. Representation is secondary to practical orientation, relevance, and action.

Third, life and mind are continuous. Basic cognition begins in the autonomous self-maintenance of living systems. Higher cognition enriches, differentiates, and socializes this basic structure; it does not appear from nowhere.

Fourth, intentionality is rooted in organismic normativity. Things matter because living beings are vulnerable, self-maintaining, and situated. Aboutness is grounded in care, need, action, and viability before it becomes symbolic reference.

Fifth, perception is active and relational. Perception is not inner image construction but skillful access to a world through embodied capacities.

Sixth, the self is enacted, not substantial. There is no independent ego behind experience. There are self-patterns: bodily, affective, social, narrative, and practical organizations that can be real without being ultimate.

Seventh, experience is methodologically irreducible. First-person experience is not outside science, but it requires disciplined methods of investigation. A mature science of mind must integrate first-person, second-person, and third-person approaches.

Eighth, knowledge is neither mirroring nor arbitrary construction. Knowing is skillful, embodied, historically situated contact with a world that is materially constrained but meaningfully enacted.

Ninth, mind is social and cultural. Human cognition is formed through interaction, language, shared practices, institutions, and histories. Intersubjectivity is not added to an already complete private mind.

Tenth, groundlessness is not nihilism. The absence of ultimate foundations does not destroy truth, agency, or ethics. It situates them within relational, embodied, fallible, and transformable forms of life.

25. Strengths of the VTR position

The first major strength is that VTR overcome the sterile opposition between reductive naturalism and anti-scientific subjectivism. They insist on science, but they refuse to let science forget experience.

The second is that they provide a plausible route for naturalizing meaning. Meaning begins in life: in the organism’s need to maintain itself, regulate itself, and distinguish what matters from what does not.

The third is that they dissolve the homunculus problem. If cognition is distributed across embodied activity, there is no need for an inner spectator who reads representations.

The fourth is that they make selfhood scientifically and existentially tractable. The self is neither a Cartesian soul nor an illusion in the trivial sense. It is an enacted organization, and therefore it can be studied, destabilized, and transformed.

The fifth is that they expand psychology. They allow cognitive science to include attention, meditation, affect, embodiment, subjectivity, intersubjectivity, and ethical transformation without abandoning rigor.

26. Main difficulties and criticisms

The first difficulty is ambiguity about “enacting a world.” If this phrase is read carelessly, it sounds like idealism: the mind creates the world. The defensible interpretation is subtler: organisms enact worlds of significance within a materially real world. Physical reality is not invented, but meaningful environments are relationally constituted.

The second difficulty is the scope of anti-representationalism. It is plausible that basic perception and action are not fundamentally representational. It is less plausible that all cognition can be explained without representations, especially mathematics, counterfactual reasoning, scientific modeling, fiction, law, and planning. A strong reconstructed VTR position should treat representations as derivative tools rather than deny them absolutely.

The third difficulty is the life-mind continuity thesis. If all living systems are cognitive, the concept of cognition may become too broad. The best response is to distinguish levels: basic sense-making, sentience, perceptual consciousness, reflective thought, linguistic cognition, and self-consciousness.

The fourth difficulty concerns first-person methods. Meditation and phenomenological training can refine experience, but they can also shape, alter, and culturally script experience. Thompson later acknowledged this problem, especially in relation to the book’s treatment of mindfulness and Buddhist philosophy.

The fifth difficulty is that VTR sometimes underdevelop social and political dimensions. Later enactivism’s work on participatory sense-making helps correct this by showing that cognition is not only organism-environment coupling but also interpersonal and institutional coupling.

The sixth difficulty is that neurophenomenology may be a methodological advance rather than a metaphysical solution to consciousness. It may help us study consciousness more rigorously without fully dissolving the explanatory gap.

27. Final assessment

Varela, Thompson, and Rosch occupy a pivotal place in late twentieth-century philosophy of mind. They are not merely contributors to embodied cognition; they are among the thinkers who changed the basic question. Instead of asking, “How does the brain represent the world?” they ask, “How does an embodied living being enact a meaningful world, and how can science study this without losing lived experience?”

Their philosophy of mind is anti-Cartesian, anti-reductionist, anti-foundationalist, and anti-representationalist in its deepest tendency. But it is not anti-scientific, anti-realist, or irrationalist. Its positive view is that mind is a living, embodied, affectively valenced, socially situated process of sense-making; that self and world arise dependently within this process; and that a complete science of mind must include disciplined investigation of experience.

The most rigorous formulation is this: mind is the self-organizing, embodied, and socially situated enactment of meaning by living beings, disclosed in lived experience and investigable only through the reciprocal discipline of biological science, phenomenology, psychology, and transformative practice.

PreviousFriston, Hohwy & Clark NextAndy Clark

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
